name: Qalupalik
description: Qalupalik (also called Qallupilluit or Kallupilluk) is a twisted, human-like sea creature that lurks just below the water's surface in Greenlandic folklore, snatching children who wander too close to the ice edge. It carries a large pouch on its back made of eider-duck feathers to store captured children. The creature has long webbed fingers for silently grabbing children from the shore or ice edge, and it emits a distinctive humming sound that parents tell children to listen for. The Qalupalik is the primary cautionary figure for Greenlandic children — it teaches respect for the dangerous waters surrounding every settlement. It has a foul smell of sulfur, wears tattered sealskin clothing, and is described as having greenish, bumpy skin like a fish mixed with a toad. The legend is still actively used by Greenlandic parents today.
appearance: Humanoid with scaly greenish-bumpy skin like a fish-toad hybrid. Long webbed fingers and toes. A hunched back carrying a large feather pouch. Wears tattered sealskin clothing. Reeks of sulfur and rotting seaweed. Eyes are large, pale, and lidless.
behavior: Preys specifically on disobedient children who go near the water alone. Uses webbed fingers to silently pull children under the ice or into the water. Stores captured children in a large pouch on its back. Emits a humming sound as it approaches.
habitat: Cold Arctic waters, beneath ice floes, along shorelines near settlements. Lurks just below the surface, hidden from view. Most active during summer when children play near the water and during spring thaw when ice edges are unstable.
cultural_significance: Qalupalik is the most active cautionary figure in modern Greenland, still used by parents today to keep children safe from the very real danger of drowning in cold Arctic waters. It is the Greenlandic equivalent of the bogeyman, specifically adapted to the marine environment.
summary: Qalupalik (also called Qallupilluit or Kallupilluk) is a twisted, human-like sea creature that lurks just below the water's surface in Greenlandic folklore, snatching children who wander too close to the ice edge
fun_fact: In some traditions, Qalupalik is said to prey specifically on disobedient children who go near the water alone.

source: Greenland mythology, oral tradition & folklore collections

name: Tupilak
description: Inuit carved figure that contains spirit of ancestor or animal. Used as guardian, can protect or attack. Made from bone, skin, hair.
appearance: Small carved figure (10-30 cm) made from bone, ivory, skin. Humanoid or animal shape. Often grotesque, with open mouth, large eyes.
behavior: Guardian spirit that can attack enemies of the family. Can be controlled by shaman. Can also be dangerous if improperly made.
cultural_significance: Inuit spiritual objects. Represent connection to ancestors and animals. Used in shamanic practices.
summary: Inuit carved figure that contains spirit of ancestor or animal
fun_fact: In some traditions, Tupilak is said to guardian spirit that can attack enemies of the family.
seo_description: Inuit carved figure that contains spirit of ancestor or animal. Used as guardian, can protect or attack. Made from bone, skin, hair.
source: Greenland mythology, oral tradition & folklore collections
